sections.title: Liver & Kidney Function
sections.story: These organs clear waste and detoxify your blood. When both liver enzymes and kidney filters sit inside their healthy bands, your body's filtration system is working well.
sections.area: How well your liver and kidneys clean and detoxify your blood.

sections.title: Minerals & Electrolytes
sections.story: These minerals power your nerves, muscles and bones. Small shifts are common and often reflect a recent meal or hydration rather than illness when kidney function is good.
sections.area: The salts and minerals that power your nerves, muscles and bones.

sections.title: Heart & Sugar Control
sections.story: This is your long-term metabolic risk picture: cholesterol, blood sugar and inflammation together signal how hard your heart is working.
sections.area: Your long-term heart and sugar picture: cholesterol, blood sugar and inflammation.

sections.title: Blood Cell Health
sections.story: Red cells carry oxygen, white cells defend you, platelets clot. Counts inside their bands mean healthy blood and immune balance.
sections.area: Your blood cells: red cells carry oxygen, white cells fight infection, platelets help clotting.

sections.title: Vitamins & Iron Stores
sections.story: Nutrients that fuel bones, immunity and blood-building. Gaps here are common and easy to correct once spotted.
sections.area: Nutrients that fuel your bones, immunity and blood-building.

sections.title: Thyroid Balance
sections.story: Your metabolic thermostat. Balanced TSH, T3 and T4 mean a well-regulated metabolism and energy level.
sections.area: Your thyroid - the gland that sets your body's energy 'speed'.

sections.title: Urine Health
sections.story: A quick screen of the urinary tract - all 'negative' results are good news, meaning no signs of infection, blood or sugar spilling.
sections.area: A quick screen of your urinary tract.

patients.name: Aditya Kharbanda
patients.headline: Most of your results look healthy - 55 of 68 are within range. 13 are worth a closer look, mainly around your liver and kidney filtering, minerals and salts, heart health and blood sugar, blood cell counts, vitamins and iron, thyroid (metabolism) and urine. Nothing here is an emergency, but it's a good idea to discuss them with your doctor.

section_summary.kidneys_liver: Mostly healthy, with 2 readings to keep an eye on: joint/crystal waste and liver enzyme balance. None are alarming on their own, but worth mentioning to your doctor.
section_summary.electrolytes: 1 mineral reading is just outside the ideal range (body water & salt balance). With healthy kidneys this is usually down to a recent meal or hydration, not illness.
section_summary.heart_meta: 5 heart/sugar readings need attention (blood fat from calories, 'bad' cholesterol (furs up arteries), triglyceride-carrying fat, all 'bad' cholesterol combined and low-grade inflammation / heart risk). These are the key numbers for your heart and diabetes risk - please discuss with your doctor.
section_summary.blood_cells: Blood counts are mostly healthy, with 1 reading just nudging the edge (clot-forming cells). Often a recent mild virus; usually nothing to worry about.
section_summary.vitamins_iron: 2 nutrient readings are below ideal (nerve & blood-cell vitamin and bone & immunity vitamin). Gaps here are common and usually easy to fix with diet or supplements - ask your doctor.
section_summary.thyroid: Thyroid reading is close to the edge (thyroid 'thermostat' signal). Usually nothing urgent, but your doctor may want to recheck.
section_summary.urine: Mostly clear, with 1 item(s) to note: blood in urine. Usually minor, but worth mentioning if you have any symptoms.

tests.name: Uric Acid
tests.note: High can cause gout or kidney stones.

tests.name: AST:ALT Ratio
tests.note: By itself this ratio rarely matters when both enzymes are normal.

tests.name: LDL Cholesterol
tests.note: Above ideal - a heart-risk marker worth a chat with your doctor.

tests.name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
tests.note: Above ideal - reflects overall heart risk.

tests.name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
tests.note: Low - weaker bones and immunity; common and fixable.

patients.name: Siddharth
patients.headline: Most of your results look healthy - 52 of 68 are within range. 16 are worth a closer look, mainly around your liver and kidney filtering, minerals and salts, heart health and blood sugar, blood cell counts, vitamins and iron, thyroid (metabolism) and urine. Nothing here is an emergency, but it's a good idea to discuss them with your doctor.

section_summary.kidneys_liver: Mostly healthy, with 2 readings to keep an eye on: kidney waste filtering and liver enzyme balance. None are alarming on their own, but worth mentioning to your doctor.
section_summary.electrolytes: 1 mineral reading is just outside the ideal range (heart & muscle cell salt). With healthy kidneys this is usually down to a recent meal or hydration, not illness.
section_summary.heart_meta: 5 heart/sugar readings need attention (blood fat from calories, 'good' cholesterol (clears arteries), 'bad' cholesterol (furs up arteries), triglyceride-carrying fat and all 'bad' cholesterol combined). These are the key numbers for your heart and diabetes risk - please discuss with your doctor.
section_summary.blood_cells: 3 blood-cell readings are outside range (oxygen-carrying pigment, variation in red-cell size and general inflammation marker). Worth checking with your doctor, especially if you feel tired or run down.
section_summary.vitamins_iron: 3 nutrient readings are low (bone & immunity vitamin, circulating iron and how full iron transport is). These can affect energy, bones and immunity, so worth correcting with your doctor's guidance.
section_summary.thyroid: Thyroid reading is close to the edge (thyroid 'thermostat' signal). Usually nothing urgent, but your doctor may want to recheck.
section_summary.urine: Mostly clear, with 1 item(s) to note: protein in urine. Usually minor, but worth mentioning if you have any symptoms.

tests.name: Creatinine
tests.note: May mean the kidneys filter a bit slower.

tests.name: AST:ALT Ratio
tests.note: By itself this ratio rarely matters when both enzymes are normal.

tests.name: HDL Cholesterol
tests.note: Lower side - raise with exercise/healthy fats.

tests.name: LDL Cholesterol
tests.note: Above ideal - a heart-risk marker worth a chat with your doctor.

tests.name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
tests.note: Above ideal - reflects overall heart risk.

tests.name: Hemoglobin
tests.note: Anaemia - can cause tiredness; check iron/B12.

tests.name: RDW
tests.note: Mixed sizes often mean iron lack.

tests.name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
tests.note: Low - weaker bones and immunity; common and fixable.

tests.name: Transferrin Saturation
tests.note: Under 20% means low usable iron - an early iron shortage.
